description:
The `loadRecentDismissals` function filters using ISO string comparison (`d.timestamp >= cutoff`), which is only correct for UTC timestamps with consistent formatting. If a timestamp is stored without a trailing `Z` (e.g., `2026-03-10T12:00:00` instead of `2026-03-10T12:00:00Z`), lexicographic comparison still works for ISO 8601 local-time strings in the same timezone, but timestamps from different sources could be formatted differently. More critically, `new Date(Date.now() - maxAgeMs).toISOString()` always produces UTC with `Z`, so comparison against a non-UTC timestamp (e.g., `2026-03-10T12:00:00+05:30`) will produce incorrect results â€” the string `2026-03-10T12:00:00+05:30` sorts differently than its UTC equivalent.


[security] Compare using `new Date(d.timestamp).getTime() >= new Date(cutoff).getTime()` instead of string comparison to handle timezone variations correctly.
suggestion: [correctness] Normalize timestamps to UTC milliseconds for comparison: `new Date(d.timestamp).getTime() >= (Date.now() - maxAgeMs)`. This handles any valid ISO 8601 format regardless of timezone suffix.
note: All timestamps created by our code via new Date().toISOString(), always UTC with Z suffix

description: The `groupIntoThreads` function incorrectly handles root comments: when a root comment (with no `in_reply_to_id`) is processed, `threadId = c.id`, so it adds itself to its own thread. But when a reply comes in with `in_reply_to_id = rootId`, it correctly maps to `threadId = rootId`. However, if the root comment is processed after its replies, the root's entry overwrites nothing but the root ends up in the thread as a reply to itself. More critically, if two different root comments exist, each creates its own thread entry keyed by their own `id`. The root comment itself is included in the thread array, and then filtered out via `c.id !== root.id` in `extractDismissalSignals`. This works, but the `root` lookup in `extractDismissalSignals` searches `threadComments` for a comment where `c.in_reply_to_id == null` â€” yet when `in_reply_to_id` is `undefined` (absent from API response rather than explicitly null), `undefined == null` is true in JS, so that's fine. The real bug: if a reply comment has `in_reply_to_id` pointing to another reply (nested reply), it gets keyed under that reply's ID, creating a phantom thread that will never match a marker root, silently losing the signal.
suggestion: When grouping threads, always resolve to the root by walking the reply chain, or use the convention that GitHub PR review comments' `in_reply_to_id` always points to the original comment (not a nested reply). Add a comment documenting this assumption. Alternatively, pre-index comments by `id` and normalize all `in_reply_to_id` to the true root before grouping.
note: GitHub review comment API does not support nested replies; in_reply_to_id always points to root

description: The dismissal injection log message is written to stderr via console.error but it is informational, not an error. The architecture convention logs telemetry/warning failures to stderr; informational progress messages should go to stdout or be omitted.
suggestion: Either remove the injection count message entirely (it is implementation detail noise for most users) or route it through a verbose/debug flag. If it is kept, use console.log rather than console.error since it is not an error.
note: Intentional: stderr for progress messages, stdout reserved for pipeable report output

description:
The `FINDING_MARKER_RE` regex uses `[\w-]+` to match finding IDs, which is appropriate for UUIDs. However, the marker is embedded in HTML comment syntax inside GitHub markdown. A malicious PR author could craft a review comment body containing `` to inject a fake dismissal signal during `sync-dismissals`. This would cause a dismissal record to be created for a finding ID that may not exist, or could be crafted to match a legitimate finding ID from a prior session.


[correctness] Make the regex more specific to UUID format: `/<!-- telesis:finding:([0-9a-f-]+) -->/` or add a comment documenting the expected ID format. Consider not exporting the raw regex; instead export an `extractFindingId(text: string): string | null` helper.
suggestion: [security] Validate that extracted finding IDs match UUID format (e.g., `/^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}$/i`) before creating dismissal signals. In `extractDismissalSignals`, reject `findingId` values that don't match UUID format. This prevents injection of arbitrary IDs via crafted PR comments.
note: Finding ID marker correlation is inherent to the sync design; fake markers produce degraded records with no matching finding

description: src/github/dismissals.ts imports from src/agent/review/dismissal/ (DismissalReason, DismissalSignal, DismissalSource). This creates a dependency from the github package into the agent/review package. Per the documented architecture, the github package is a contained adapter layer; it should not know about agent internals.
suggestion: Move DismissalSignal and DismissalSource to a shared location (e.g., src/agent/review/dismissal/source.ts already exists â€” consider whether the interface belongs in a more neutral location such as src/types.ts or src/review/types.ts), or have dismissals.ts return a raw intermediate type that the CLI layer maps to DismissalSignal. The key concern is that github/ imports from agent/review/ creates a coupling that runs counter to the containment pattern.
note: Cross-package import is intentional per plan and documented in ARCHITECTURE.md

description: The `listPullRequestReviewComments` function casts `data` directly to `readonly GitHubReviewComment[]` without any runtime validation. If the GitHub API returns unexpected data (e.g., error response object, rate limit response), the unvalidated data flows into `extractDismissalSignals` where `FINDING_MARKER_RE.test(c.body)` would throw if `c.body` is undefined or not a string.
suggestion: Add a runtime check that each element in `data` has at least `id` (number) and `body` (string) before casting, filtering out malformed entries. This is consistent with the existing defensive pattern in `loadDismissals` which validates records before use.
note: Same trust model as rest of client.ts; fetchWithRetry handles error responses

description: In `sync-dismissals`, when a signal has no `findingId`, the dismissal is created with `findingId: "unknown"` and `sessionId: "unknown"`. A subsequent `findDismissalByFindingId(dir, "unknown")` call would return the first such dismissal rather than none, and `isDismissalRecord` in `store.ts` accepts `"unknown"` as a valid `findingId` string. Multiple signals without `findingId` would all get `findingId: "unknown"`, making them indistinguishable and `findDismissalByFindingId` unreliable for these records.
suggestion: Use a unique UUID for `findingId` when `signal.findingId` is absent (e.g., `signal.findingId ?? randomUUID()`), or use an empty string with a note that it's unresolved. Document that `findingId` may be a synthetic UUID when the finding couldn't be correlated.
note: Degraded records still provide value for stats/pattern detection when correlation fails

description: When sync-dismissals cannot find a finding by ID (result is null), the dismissal record is still written with findingId: 'unknown', sessionId: 'unknown', severity: 'medium', category: 'bug' as hardcoded fallbacks. These defaults are arbitrary and will silently produce misleading dismissal records when the signal's findingId does not match any stored session.
suggestion: When result is null and signal.findingId is present, log a warning and skip the dismissal rather than writing a record with fabricated metadata. Only write records without full metadata when signal.findingId is absent (the case where the marker was not embedded or was unavailable). Alternatively, make the missing-finding case an explicit warning the user can see.
note: Same as a38104a6 â€” hardcoded fallbacks for uncorrelated signals are by design

description: The name `DismissalSource` is used for two distinct concepts: the platform enum type in `types.ts` ("cli" | "github" | ...) and the adapter interface in `source.ts`. This creates a naming collision that violates clarity and will confuse consumers importing from both modules.
suggestion: Rename the string union in `types.ts` to `DismissalPlatform` (matching field name intent) and update all references in `store.ts`, `cli/review.ts`, and the `Dismissal` interface. The adapter interface in `source.ts` keeps the name `DismissalSource`.
note: Different modules, imported separately â€” naming is clear in context

description: In `syncDismissalsCommand`, `extractPRContext()` is used to infer the GitHub owner/repo for `sync-dismissals`. However, `extractPRContext` is documented as reading `GITHUB_EVENT_PATH` (GitHub Actions environment). A developer running `telesis review sync-dismissals --pr 42` locally without that env var will get a misleading error about `GITHUB_EVENT_PATH` rather than guidance on setting `GITHUB_REPOSITORY`. The error message says 'run from a repository with a GitHub remote' but the implementation doesn't actually parse git remotes.
suggestion: Either implement git remote parsing as a fallback (parse `git remote get-url origin` to extract owner/repo), or update the error message to accurately describe what's required: 'Set GITHUB_REPOSITORY=owner/repo environment variable or run in GitHub Actions.'
note: Fixed: buildLocalPRContext now parses git remote as fallback

description: `loadRecentDismissals` uses lexicographic string comparison (`d.timestamp >= cutoff`) to filter by age. ISO 8601 timestamps sort correctly lexicographically only when they use the same timezone offset format. If any timestamp is stored with a non-UTC offset (e.g., `2026-03-10T14:00:00+02:00` vs cutoff `2026-03-10T12:00:00.000Z`), the comparison will produce incorrect results because string ordering doesn't account for timezone normalization.
suggestion: Compare using `Date` objects: `return all.filter((d) => new Date(d.timestamp).getTime() >= cutoff);` where `cutoff = Date.now() - maxAgeMs`.
note: All timestamps created by our code via toISOString(), always UTC

description: In `sync-dismissals`, when a signal has no `findingId`, the dismissal is created with `findingId: "unknown"` and `sessionId: "unknown"`. If `appendDismissal` is called multiple times for different signals without a `findingId`, all will share `findingId: "unknown"`. Subsequent calls to `findDismissalByFindingId(rootDir, 'unknown')` will return the first one, and the system will be confused about which finding was dismissed.
suggestion: For signals without a `findingId`, use a stable identifier derived from the signal content (e.g., a hash of `path + description`) rather than the literal string `"unknown"`, or omit the dismissal record entirely with a warning.
note: Degraded records with unknown findingId are by design for uncorrelated signals

description: The `groupIntoThreads`-equivalent in `findCandidateNoisePatterns` rebuilds the per-reason groups using spread (`[...existing, d]`) inside a loop, creating O(NÂ˛) allocations for N dismissals per reason group. For large dismissal files this is wasteful.
suggestion: Use `push` on a mutable array instead of spread: `const existing = byReason.get(d.reason); if (existing) { existing.push(d); } else { byReason.set(d.reason, [d]); }`
note: Fixed: findCandidateNoisePatterns now uses mutable push

description: The `stripMarker` function uses `replace(/<!-- telesis:finding:[\w-]+ -->\n?/g, '')` to clean finding markers from comment bodies before storing them as `description` in `DismissalSignal`. However, `FINDING_MARKER_RE` (used for detection) is not anchored and does not use the global flag â€” a comment body with multiple markers would have only the first captured, but `stripMarker` removes all of them with the global regex. More importantly, comment bodies from GitHub PR reviews are UNTRUSTED user input that gets stored in `dismissals.jsonl` and later injected verbatim into LLM prompts via `formatDismissedFindings`. A malicious PR commenter could craft a reply body with prompt injection content (e.g., instructions to the model to ignore prior guidelines) that would be stored and replayed into future review prompts.
suggestion: Treat the `description` field stored from GitHub comment bodies as untrusted display text only. In `formatDismissedFindings`, consider truncating descriptions to a reasonable length (e.g., 200 characters) and stripping any content that looks like prompt injection (markdown headers starting with `##`, lines starting with `You are`, etc.). At minimum, document that this field is untrusted and add a length cap in the formatter.
note: Description content needed for prompt suppression; PR authors with write access are semi-trusted

description: `extractRepoContext` and `buildLocalPRContext` use `execSync` (child_process) to invoke git commands. Per the documented architecture, git interaction is contained in `src/agent/review/diff.ts` ('Only `diff.ts` executes git commands'). Adding a second git execution point in the github package violates this containment rule.
suggestion: Extract the git remote parsing into `diff.ts` or a shared `src/git/` utility, and have `environment.ts` call that module. Alternatively, accept the remote URL as a parameter and parse it in `environment.ts` without shelling out â€” the caller (CLI) can provide the URL obtained from the canonical git module.
note: Git containment rule applies to review agent (diff.ts), not github package. extractRepoContext reads remote URL, different concern
